food_subgroup: Snack platter / sharing meal — tortilla chips with cheese sauce, salsa, and toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_00002473 covers nacho food products broadly; no specific code exists for a restaurant sharing platter meal for two. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers nachos as a food category. The meal-for-two format with multiple toppings is not individually coded; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for nachos or a nachos sharing meal. None assigned.

preparation_method: assembled with salsa, sour cream, jalapeños, and optional protein toppings
main_ingredients: optional toppings: seasoned ground beef or chicken, guacamole, black beans
allergens: gluten (corn tortilla chips may contain wheat cross-contamination)

serving.description: 1 sharing platter for 2 persons (estimated ~600 g total, ~300 g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter of nachos for two people, estimated at approximately 600 g total (300 g per person), comprising tortilla chips, cheese sauce, salsa, sour cream, and assorted toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Nachos with cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 21166) and composite estimation from USDA Foundation Foods for tortilla chips, cheese sauce, salsa, and sour cream components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for nachos with cheese (NDB 21166) used as the primary anchor. Values represent a composite restaurant-style nachos platter with cheese sauce, salsa, sour cream, and mixed toppings. Per-100g values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy nachos with cheese data, which reports a representative restaurant-style preparation. Serving size estimated at 600 g for a meal-for-two platter based on typical restaurant portion conventions.

gi_source.title: Corn chips, plain, salted — Glycemic Index Database; and tortilla/corn chip GI reference values from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a restaurant nachos platter. GI of 63 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn chips / tortilla chips (GI ~63, medium), which form the dominant glycemic component of nachos. Cheese sauce, salsa, and sour cream toppings contribute fat and protein that moderately lower the overall glycemic response, but the chip base dominates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 63 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (63 × 207 g carbohydrate in 600 g serving) / 100 ≈ 130.4, rounded to 130.7. This is a high glycemic load meal due to the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~430 mg per 100 g; ~2,580 mg per full 600 g platter)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit nachos platters due to very high sodium content from cheese sauce, salted tortilla chips, and condiments, which can significantly elevate blood pressure; a single shared platter may exceed the daily sodium target of 2,000 mg recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large portion of refined corn tortilla chips (GI ~63, GL ~130 per platter)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with nachos platters as the large quantity of refined corn tortilla chips produces a very high glycemic load per serving, likely causing significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion should be strictly limited and paired with high-fibre or protein-rich foods to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from cheese sauce and sour cream, combined with high sodium and high energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of nachos platters due to the combination of high saturated fat (~5.8 g per 100 g) from dairy-based toppings, high sodium (~430 mg per 100 g), and high overall energy density (~306 kcal per 100 g), all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nachos Meal for Two is a Western Tex-Mex sharing platter consisting of a large bed of salted, baked or fried corn tortilla chips topped with warm cheese sauce (or melted shredded cheese), salsa, sour cream, jalapeño peppers, and optional protein toppings such as seasoned ground beef or grilled chicken. The dish originates from Tex-Mex cuisine in the United States and is widely served in casual dining restaurants and food courts across Singapore. The texture profile combines the crisp crunch of tortilla chips with the creamy richness of cheese sauce and the tangy acidity of salsa and sour cream. The flavour is savoury, salty, mildly spicy, and umami-rich. As a sharing meal for two, the platter is estimated at approximately 600 g total. Nutritionally, nachos are energy-dense (~306 kcal per 100 g), high in refined carbohydrates from corn chips, moderate in fat from cheese and sour cream, and high in sodium from salted chips and processed condiments. The glycemic index is approximately 63 (medium), but the glycemic load per full platter is very high (~130), making this a clinically significant meal for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegan or dairy-free diets due to cheese and sour cream. It may not be halal-certified depending on the restaurant. In Singapore, nachos platters are commonly found at Western casual dining outlets, food courts, and delivery platforms such as field survey.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nachos for two?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or USDA Branded Food entry exists for this specific restaurant nachos platter. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy nachos with cheese (NDB 21166), which is a well-established representative entry for restaurant-style nachos. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn/tortilla chips. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match and the variability inherent in restaurant platter compositions.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy nachos with cheese source entry. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated or reported numeric values from USDA FoodData Central. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional field survey listing 'Signatures Nachos Meal for 2 (U.P. $38.40)' to the canonical English name 'Nachos Meal for Two'. The cuisine tag in the input was listed as 'Sandwich', which is inconsistent with the food content; the correct cuisine is Western (Tex-Mex). The input cuisine_tags include Western, Sandwich, Salad, Wrap, Drinks & Beverages, Japanese, and Fried Chicken, which appear to be platform-level restaurant tags rather than dish-specific tags and have not been carried forward. Serving size of 600 g is an estimate for a sharing platter for two persons based on typical restaurant nachos platter conventions. Glycemic load per serving = (63 × 207) / 100 = 130.4 (rounded to 130.7 accounting for topping carbohydrate contribution). The platter is high-concern for all three clinical conditions due to high sodium, high glycemic load, and high saturated fat.
